overview.body: This is a MASTER week — the final polish on your Module 3 game! You'll generate a professional trip report, sort your hazards alphabetically, count unique cities visited, and save everything to a file. By the end, your road trip will have a permanent record worth sharing.

mentorQuote.quote: A trip report isn't just data — it's a keepsake! Think about what players want to remember: the cities they visited, the hazards they survived, the miles they conquered. Present it beautifully with headers, sorted lists, and clear formatting. When they open that file later, they should relive the adventure!

objectives: HAZARD_NAMES List — Random hazard selection using list indexing (replaces if/elif)
Sorted Hazards — Display hazards A-Z using .copy() and .sort()
Unique Cities — Count distinct cities using the in operator
File Output — Save a formatted trip report to trip_report.txt
overview.keyConceptsText: Chapter 7: 7.4 (in operator), 7.5 (sort method, len function). Chapter 6: 6.1 (Writing to files).
overview.textbookReference: Starting Out with Python, 6th Edition by Tony Gaddis — Chapters 6 & 7
programRequirements: HAZARD_NAMES list replaces if/elif chain for hazard selection
get_random_hazard() function uses list indexing with random
Hazards must be displayed in alphabetical order
Unique cities count uses the in operator (no duplicates)
overview.filenameNote: Submit TWO files this week: function_library.py — Your expanded module (now with list utilities) and lab-3-3.py — Your polished game with trip report and file output.
overview.additionalFilesNote: function_library.py is submitted alongside lab-3-3.py this week — it gets the display_numbered_list() function. CodeGrade tests this function in isolation.
overview.saveYourCode: Keep BOTH files! In Module 4, you'll add dictionaries for city data and sets for tracking unique items more elegantly. Your function library will keep growing!

rows.task: Add hazard list and get_random_hazard() function
Copy and sort hazards list for report
Build unique cities list using in operator

timeGuide.strategy: Always use .copy() before .sort()! If you sort your original list, you'll lose the order in which hazards occurred. Make a copy first, then sort the copy.

beforeYouBegin.intro: Most of this week's work lives AFTER the game loop. Here's the full picture — [NEW] tags show what you're adding.
beforeYouBegin.architecture:
# ---- CONSTANTS ----
HAZARD_NAMES = ["Buc-ees appears!", "Armadillo crossing!", ...]  [NEW — replaces if/elif]
REPORT_FILE  = 'trip_report.txt'                                  [NEW]

# ---- FUNCTION LIBRARY ----
display_numbered_list(items, title)   [NEW in function_library.py]

# ---- HELPER FUNCTIONS ----
get_random_hazard()   uses HAZARD_NAMES[random index]  [NEW — replaces if/elif]

# ---- GAME LOOP (mostly unchanged from 3.2) ----

GAME LOOP:
│
│── Display menu, get choice, Destination Gate
│
│   TRAVEL LOOP:
│   │── Roll D20, move miles
│   │── Check for hazard
│   │      If hazard: get_random_hazard()  [REPLACED]
│   │── Repeat until arrival
│
│── Append city, update totals, check outcome

# ---- AFTER THE LOOP (this is where the action is) ----

FINAL TRIP REPORT:
│
│── Show visited cities (display_numbered_list)
│
│── Build unique_cities list:                        [NEW]
│      for city in visited_cities:
│          if city not in unique_cities: append it
│
│── Sort hazards:                                      [NEW]
│      sorted_hazards = hazards_encountered.copy()
│      sorted_hazards.sort()
│      Display with display_numbered_list
│
│── Show trip stats (miles, turns, escape rate)
│
│── Ask: Save report to file?                          [NEW]
│      If yes: write everything to trip_report.txt

"Thanks for playing!"

checkpoints.title: Add HAZARD_NAMES List
checkpoints.subtitle: Replace the if/elif chain for hazard selection with a list and random indexing.

content.body:
Before you start:
  1. Copy your Lab 3.2 files — You'll build on your existing game
  2. Rename the main file to lab-3-3.py
  3. Test your Lab 3.2 code — Make sure save/load still works before adding features

content.quote: This is polish week. The game loop barely changes — most of what you're building lives in the trip report at the end. The one change inside the loop is swapping that old hazard if/elif for a clean list lookup. Everything else is about making the FINAL output shine: sorted hazards, unique cities, and saving it all to a file.

content.body: A constant list of hazard names and a function that picks one randomly using list indexing.

Replacing if/elif chains with lists and random indexing is how games scale. Adding 10 more hazards to a list takes seconds; adding 10 more elif branches is tedious and error-prone. This pattern is used in every game for inventory items, enemy types, loot tables, and more.

items: Near the top of your file (with other constants), add this HAZARD_NAMES list:
Create a get_random_hazard() function with no parameters
Use random.randint(0, len(HAZARD_NAMES) - 1) to get a random index
Return HAZARD_NAMES[index]
Replace the old if/elif hazard chain with a call to this function

content.code:
HAZARD_NAMES = [
    "Buc-ee's appears!",
    "Armadillo crossing!",
    "Speed trap!",
    "Check engine light!",
    "Whataburger cravings!",
    "Toll road confusion!"
]

references.description: Shows creating a list with initial values.
Demonstrates len() for list size — use it to calculate valid index range.

rows: List indices start at 0, so index 6 doesn't exist in a 6-item list
If you add more hazards later, you'll get wrong results. Use len() - 1
Function returns None, you get no hazard displayed

content.body: Step 1: Add this temporary test code at the bottom of your file:

content.code:
# TEMPORARY TEST - DELETE BEFORE SUBMITTING
print("Testing get_random_hazard():")
for i in range(10):
    print(f"  {i + 1}. {get_random_hazard()}")

content.body: Step 2: Run your program. You should see output like:

content.output:
Testing get_random_hazard():
  1. Buc-ee's appears!
  2. Speed trap!
  3. Armadillo crossing!
  4. Whataburger cravings!
  5. Buc-ee's appears!
  6. Toll road confusion!
  7. Check engine light!
  8. Speed trap!
  9. Armadillo crossing!
  10. Whataburger cravings!

content.body: Step 3: Verify these critical behaviors: Random selection — hazards vary between runs. No crashes — no IndexError from invalid indices. All hazards appear — run several times to see variety.
Step 4: Once testing passes, delete the test code and move on to Checkpoint 2.

items: HAZARD_NAMES list with 6 Texas-themed hazards
get_random_hazard() returns a random hazard from the list
No more if/elif chain for hazard selection

checkpoints.title: Add display_numbered_list() to Library
checkpoints.subtitle: Expand your function library with a reusable list display function.

content.body: A function called display_numbered_list(items, title) that prints a list with numbers and a title.

items: Open function_library.py
Add a comment section: # LIST UTILITY FUNCTIONS (Added in Lab 3-3)
Define display_numbered_list(items, title) with two parameters
Print the title, then a dashed line using '-' * len(title)
Use a for loop with range(len(items)) to print numbered items
Add a docstring describing the function

references.description: Shows the for index in range(len(list)) pattern for accessing items with their positions.

rows: You get items but not their index — can't print numbers
Forgetting the blank line at the end
Output runs together with next section

content.body: Step 1: Add this temporary test code in your library's main():

content.code:
# TEMPORARY TEST - DELETE BEFORE SUBMITTING
test_cities = ['San Antonio', 'Austin', 'Houston']
display_numbered_list(test_cities, 'CITIES VISITED:')

content.body: Step 2: Run function_library.py directly. You should see:

content.output:
CITIES VISITED:
----------------
  1. San Antonio
  2. Austin
  3. Houston

content.body: Step 3: Verify these behaviors: Title appears with dashed underline matching title length. Numbering starts at 1, not 0. Blank line appears after the list.
Step 4: Once testing passes, move on to Checkpoint 3.

items: function_library.py with display_numbered_list()
Function prints title, dashed line, numbered items, blank line
A docstring describing the function

checkpoints.title: Sorted Hazards Display
checkpoints.subtitle: Copy and sort the hazards list for alphabetical display in your trip report.

content.body: A sorted copy of your hazards list that displays alphabetically (A-Z) in the trip report.

items: Find your trip report section (after the game loop ends)
Before displaying hazards, create a copy: sorted_hazards = hazards_list.copy()
Sort the copy: sorted_hazards.sort()
Display the sorted list (either with your new function or manually)

references.description: Shows list operations in practice. The sort() method is covered in Section 7.5 of the textbook — it modifies the list in place. That's why we copy first!

rows: Both variables point to same list — sorting one sorts both!
Destroys original order — can't show hazards in occurrence order elsewhere
sort() returns None, not the sorted list — you'll lose your data

content.body: Step 1: Add this temporary test code to verify copy/sort behavior:

content.code:
# TEMPORARY TEST - DELETE BEFORE SUBMITTING
test_hazards = ["Speed trap!", "Buc-ee's appears!", "Armadillo crossing!"]
sorted_test = test_hazards.copy()
sorted_test.sort()
print("Original:", test_hazards)
print("Sorted:", sorted_test)

content.body: Step 2: Run your program. You should see:

content.output:
Original: ["Speed trap!", "Buc-ee's appears!", "Armadillo crossing!"]
Sorted: ["Armadillo crossing!", "Buc-ee's appears!", "Speed trap!"]

content.body: Step 3: Verify these behaviors: Original unchanged — still in occurrence order. Sorted is A-Z — alphabetical order. Two separate lists — different objects in memory.
Step 4: Play your game, encounter some hazards, and verify the trip report shows them sorted.

items: Trip report shows hazards in alphabetical order
Original hazards_list is NOT modified
Uses .copy() then .sort() pattern

checkpoints.title: Unique Cities Count
checkpoints.subtitle: Count distinct cities visited using the in operator.

content.body: A list of unique cities (no duplicates) and a count to display in your trip report.

items: In your trip report section, create an empty list: unique_cities = []
Loop through visited_cities with a for loop
For each city, check: if city not in unique_cities:
If not found, append it: unique_cities.append(city)
Display the count using len(unique_cities)

references.description: Demonstrates the in operator to check if an item exists in a list. Combine with not to check if absent.

rows: Logic is backwards — you only add if it's NOT already there
That's total visits, not unique cities — includes duplicates

content.body: Step 1: Add this temporary test code to verify the pattern:

content.code:
# TEMPORARY TEST - DELETE BEFORE SUBMITTING
test_route = ['San Antonio', 'Austin', 'San Antonio', 'Houston', 'Austin']
unique = []
for city in test_route:
    if city not in unique:
        unique.append(city)
print(f"Route: {test_route}")
print(f"Unique: {unique}")
print(f"Unique count: {len(unique)}")

content.body: Step 2: Run your program. You should see:

content.output:
Route: ['San Antonio', 'Austin', 'San Antonio', 'Houston', 'Austin']
Unique: ['San Antonio', 'Austin', 'Houston']
Unique count: 3

content.body: Step 3: Verify these behaviors: 5 visits in route, but only 3 unique cities. Duplicates filtered — San Antonio and Austin appear once each. Order preserved — first occurrence order maintained.
Step 4: Play your game, backtrack to some cities, and verify the unique count is correct.

items: Trip report shows "Unique cities visited: X"
Count excludes duplicate visits
Uses the not in pattern

checkpoints.title: Save Trip Report to File
checkpoints.subtitle: Write a formatted trip report to trip_report.txt.

content.quote: This is the finish line! Take all that data you've been tracking — cities, hazards, stats — and write it to a file the player can keep. Open that file in Notepad after you play. If it looks good there, it's good anywhere!

content.body: A save_trip_report() function that writes a formatted report to a text file.

items: Define a save_trip_report() function with parameters for all report data
Open trip_report.txt in write mode: open(REPORT_FILE, 'w')
Write header: title, separator line, driver name, destination
Write route using ' -> '.join(visited_cities)
Write statistics: miles, turns, hazards faced/escaped, escape rate
Write sorted hazards list (if any)
Close the file and confirm to user

content.output:
DEEP IN THE HEART - TRIP REPORT
========================================
Driver: Maria
Final Destination: Galveston

ROUTE:
  San Antonio -> Houston -> Galveston
  Unique cities: 3

STATISTICS:
  Total Miles: 267
  Total Turns: 4
  Hazards Faced: 2
  Hazards Escaped: 1
  Escape Rate: 50.0%

HAZARDS (A-Z):
  - Armadillo crossing!
  - Buc-ee's appears!

========================================

references.description: Shows writing to files with outfile.write(). Remember to add '\n' for newlines!

rows: All text runs together on one line
Output goes to screen, not file
Data may not be written completely

content.body: Step 1: Play a complete game to the beach.
Step 2: When prompted, choose to save the trip report.
Step 3: Open trip_report.txt in Notepad or VS Code. Verify: Header with title and separator. Route shows all cities with arrows. Statistics are accurate. Hazards are sorted A-Z.
Step 4: Play again with different choices and verify report updates correctly.

items: save_trip_report() function writes formatted report
trip_report.txt contains complete trip summary
User is prompted to save at end of game

beforeYouSubmit: Both files have proper docstrings with your name
No test files or temporary test code being submitted
finalChecklist.scopeReminder: You may use .copy(), .sort(), and the in operator. Do NOT use list slicing [start:end], list comprehensions, readlines(), or sets (those are Module 4 topics).

specific: All previous techniques (variables, if/else, loops, functions, file I/O, lists)
Writing to text files for report generation
permittedTechniques.notPermitted: list slicing, list comprehensions, readlines(), sets, dictionaries

finalChecklist.academicIntegrity: This assignment must be your own work. You may reference the textbook and BookEx examples. You may NOT copy code from others, use AI, or share your code.

ifYouGetStuck: Re-read Program 7-2 (in_list.py) and Section 7.5 — the in operator and sort() patterns
Test copy/sort in isolation — make sure .copy() works before using in your code
Check your file output — open trip_report.txt in Notepad to see what's really there
Submit to CodeGrade early — shows exactly which tests pass or fail
Come to Zoom or office hours — screen share and debug together
needHelp.bookexResourceNote: Chapter 6 & 7 BookEx Reference Guides — step-by-step breakdowns of every textbook example
